provenance: Names are demographic SIGNALS for a synthetic counterfactual audit in the Bertrand-Mullainathan (2004) "Are Emily and Greg More Employable than Lakisha and Jamal?" tradition; they are attached to otherwise-identical profiles so that any outcome difference is attributable to the perceived race x gender the name evokes, not to any other attribute. Sources of the name coding: (1) Bertrand & Mullainathan (2004) directly supply the white-coded set (Greg, Brad, Geoffrey, Matthew, Jay; Emily, Anne, Allison, Jill, Kristen) and the Black-coded set (Jamal, DeShawn, Tyrone, Darnell, Leroy; Lakisha, Tamika, Ebony, Latoya, Aisha), drawn from Massachusetts birth-certificate frequency data on names most distinctive by race. (2) Surnames reinforce the signal: high-frequency Anglo surnames (Walsh, Baker, Sullivan, O'Brien) for white cells; common Black-associated surnames (Washington, Jackson, Booker, Jefferson) for Black cells; and the most frequent U.S. Hispanic surnames per Census Bureau data (Rodriguez, Hernandez, Gonzalez, Ramirez, Morales) for Hispanic cells. (3) Hispanic first names follow later correspondence-audit and Census-informed practice (e.g., Jose, Juan, Luis, Miguel, Carlos; Maria, Guadalupe/Lupe, Sofia, Isabella), since B-M did not include a Hispanic cell. IMPORTANT CAVEAT: name-based race/gender coding is an imperfect, probabilistic proxy. Real individuals of every group bear every one of these names; the coding reflects population-level frequency distributions and perceived associations, not biological or definitional truth. Some names (especially Hispanic ones) also carry confounded signals of nativity, class, or generation. Use only in aggregate as a manipulated treatment, interpret effects as the causal impact of a perceived-demographic name cue rather than of race or gender themselves, and pair with robustness checks across alternative name sets.
